datetime module
datetime::epoch_millis

Get the Unix epoch timestamp in milliseconds from a datetime.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
datetime::epoch_millis(datetime_value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
datetime_value	Datetime	The datetime to convert to epoch milliseconds
Return Value

Returns an integer representing the number of milliseconds since the Unix epoch.

Examples
$ Get epoch milliseconds from datetime
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {datetime::epoch_millis(cast('2024-03-15T14:30:45Z', datetime))}
$ ctrl+enter to run

datetime module
datetime::format

Format a datetime as a string.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
datetime::format(datetime_value, format_string)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
datetime_value	Datetime	The datetime to format
format_string	Utf8	The format string pattern
Return Value

Returns a Utf8 representing the formatted datetime.

Examples
$ Format datetime as string
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {datetime::format(cast('2024-03-15T14:30:45Z', datetime), '%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ')}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
date::format
time::format

datetime module
datetime::from_epoch

Create a datetime from a Unix epoch timestamp in seconds.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
datetime::from_epoch(seconds)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
seconds	Integer	The number of seconds since the Unix epoch
Return Value

Returns a Datetime corresponding to the given epoch timestamp in seconds.

Examples
$ Create datetime from epoch seconds
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {datetime::from_epoch(1710513045)}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run

datetime::from_epoch_millis

datetime module
datetime::from_epoch_millis

Create a datetime from a Unix epoch timestamp in milliseconds.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
datetime::from_epoch_millis(milliseconds)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
milliseconds	Integer	The number of milliseconds since the Unix epoch
Return Value

Returns a Datetime corresponding to the given epoch timestamp in milliseconds.

Examples
$ Create datetime from epoch milliseconds
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {datetime::from_epoch_millis(1710513045000)}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
datetime::epoch_millis
datetime::from_epoch

datetime module
datetime::hour

Extract the hour (0-23) from a datetime.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
datetime::hour(datetime_value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
datetime_value	Datetime	The datetime to extract the hour from
Return Value

Returns an integer representing the hour (0-23).

Examples
$ Extract hour from datetime
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {datetime::hour(cast('2024-03-15T14:30:00Z', datetime))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run

datetime module
datetime::nanosecond

Extract the nanosecond component from a datetime.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
datetime::nanosecond(datetime_value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
datetime_value	Datetime	The datetime to extract the nanosecond from
Return Value

Returns an integer representing the nanosecond component.

Examples
$ Extract nanosecond from datetime
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {datetime::nanosecond(cast('2024-03-15T14:30:45.123456789Z', datetime))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run

datetime module
datetime::new

Create a new datetime from a date and time.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
datetime::new(date, time)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
date	Date	The date component
time	Time	The time component
Return Value

Returns a Datetime constructed from the specified date and time.

Examples
$ Create new datetime from date and time
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {datetime::new(cast('2024-03-15', date), cast('14:30:45', time))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
datetime::now
datetime::from_epoch

datetime module
datetime::now

Get the current datetime.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
datetime::now()
Parameters

This function takes no parameters.

Return Value

Returns a Datetime representing the current date and time.

Examples
$ Get current datetime
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {datetime::now()}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run

datetime module
datetime::quarter

Get the quarter (1-4) from a datetime.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
datetime::quarter(datetime_value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
datetime_value	Datetime	The datetime to get the quarter from
Return Value

Returns an integer representing the quarter (1-4).

Examples
$ Get quarter from datetime
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {datetime::quarter(cast('2024-03-15T14:30:00Z', datetime))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run

datetime module
datetime::subtract

Subtract a duration from a datetime.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
datetime::subtract(datetime_value, duration)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
datetime_value	Datetime	The datetime to subtract the duration from
duration	Duration	The duration to subtract
Return Value

Returns a Datetime representing the result of subtracting the duration from the datetime.

Examples
$ Subtract duration from datetime
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {datetime::subtract(cast('2024-03-15T14:30:00Z', datetime), cast('P10D', duration))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
datetime::add
datetime::diff

datetime module
datetime::trunc

Truncate a datetime to a specified precision.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
datetime::trunc(datetime_value, precision)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
datetime_value	Datetime	The datetime to truncate
precision	Utf8	The precision to truncate to (e.g. "hour", "day")
Return Value

Returns a Datetime truncated to the specified precision.

Examples
$ Truncate datetime to hour
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {datetime::trunc(cast('2024-03-15T14:30:45Z', datetime), 'hour')}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
datetime::date
datetime::time

datetime module
datetime::week

Get the ISO week number (1-53) from a datetime.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
datetime::week(datetime_value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
datetime_value	Datetime	The datetime to get the ISO week number from
Return Value

Returns an integer representing the ISO week number (1-53).

Examples
$ Get week number from datetime
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {datetime::week(cast('2024-03-15T14:30:00Z', datetime))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
datetime::quarter
datetime::day_of_week

datetime module
datetime::year

Extract the year from a datetime.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
datetime::year(datetime_value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
datetime_value	Datetime	The datetime to extract the year from
Return Value

Returns an integer representing the year.

Examples
$ Extract year from datetime
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {datetime::year(cast('2024-03-15T14:30:00Z', datetime))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run

duration Module

Create, manipulate, and format durations representing spans of time.

Constructors
duration::years

Create a duration of the specified number of years.

duration::months

Create a duration of the specified number of months.

duration::weeks

Create a duration of the specified number of weeks.

duration::days

Create a duration of the specified number of days.

duration::hours

Create a duration of the specified number of hours.

duration::minutes

Create a duration of the specified number of minutes.

duration::seconds

Create a duration of the specified number of seconds.

duration::millis

Create a duration of the specified number of milliseconds.

Component Extraction
duration::get_months

Get the months component of a duration.

duration::get_days

Get the days component of a duration.

duration::get_nanos

Get the nanoseconds component of a duration.

Arithmetic
duration::add

Add two durations together.

duration::subtract

Subtract one duration from another.

duration::negate

Negate a duration (flip its sign).

duration::scale

Scale a duration by a numeric factor.

Formatting
duration::trunc

Truncate a duration to a specified precision.

duration::format

Format a duration as a human-readable string.

Function Reference
Function	Description
duration::years	Create a duration of the specified number of years.
duration::months	Create a duration of the specified number of months.
duration::weeks	Create a duration of the specified number of weeks.
duration::days	Create a duration of the specified number of days.
duration::hours	Create a duration of the specified number of hours.
duration::minutes	Create a duration of the specified number of minutes.
duration::seconds	Create a duration of the specified number of seconds.
duration::millis	Create a duration of the specified number of milliseconds.
duration::get_months	Get the months component of a duration.
duration::get_days	Get the days component of a duration.
duration::get_nanos	Get the nanoseconds component of a duration.
duration::add	Add two durations together.
duration::subtract	Subtract one duration from another.
duration::negate	Negate a duration (flip its sign).
duration::scale	Scale a duration by a numeric factor.
duration::trunc	Truncate a duration to a specified precision.
duration::format	Format a duration as a human-readable string.

duration module
duration::add

Add two durations together.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
duration::add(a, b)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
a	Duration	The first duration.
b	Duration	The second duration.
Return Value

Returns a Duration representing the sum of the two durations.

Examples
$ Add two durations
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {duration::add(duration::hours(1), duration::minutes(30))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
duration::subtract
duration::scale

duration module
duration::days

Create a duration of the specified number of days.

Alias: duration::day

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
duration::days(count)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
count	Integer	The number of days for the duration.
Return Value

Returns a Duration representing the specified number of days.

Examples
$ Create a duration of days
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {duration::days(10)}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
duration::hours
duration::weeks

duration module
duration::format

Format a duration as a human-readable string.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
duration::format(duration, format_string)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
duration	Duration	The duration to format.
format_string	Utf8	The format string to use.
Return Value

Returns a Utf8 with the duration formatted according to the format string.

Examples
$ Format a duration
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {duration::format(duration::months(27), '%Y years %M months')}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
duration::trunc

duration module
duration::get_days

Get the days component of a duration.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
duration::get_days(duration)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
duration	Duration	The duration to extract the days component from.
Return Value

Returns an Integer representing the days component of the duration.

Examples
$ Get days from duration
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {duration::get_days(cast('P10D', duration))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
duration::get_months
duration::get_nanos

$ RQL
Copy
1
duration::get_nanos(duration)
Parameters

duration module
duration::negate

Negate a duration (flip its sign).

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
duration::negate(duration)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
duration	Duration	The duration to negate.
Return Value

Returns a Duration with its sign flipped.

Examples
$ Negate a duration
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {duration::negate(duration::hours(2))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
duration::subtract
duration::scale

duration module
duration::scale

Scale a duration by a numeric factor.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
duration::scale(duration, factor)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
duration	Duration	The duration to scale.
factor	Number	The numeric factor to scale by.
Return Value

Returns a Duration scaled by the given factor.

Examples
$ Scale a duration
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {duration::scale(duration::hours(2), 3)}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
duration::add
duration::negate

duration module
duration::seconds

Create a duration of the specified number of seconds.

Alias: duration::second

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
duration::seconds(count)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
count	Integer	The number of seconds for the duration.
Return Value

Returns a Duration representing the specified number of seconds.

Examples
$ Create a duration of seconds
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {duration::seconds(90)}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run

duration module
duration::subtract

Subtract one duration from another.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
duration::subtract(a, b)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
a	Duration	The duration to subtract from.
b	Duration	The duration to subtract.
Return Value

Returns a Duration representing the difference between the two durations.

Examples
$ Subtract two durations
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {duration::subtract(duration::hours(2), duration::minutes(30))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
duration::add
duration::negate

duration module
duration::trunc

Truncate a duration to a specified precision.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
duration::trunc(duration, precision)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
duration	Duration	The duration to truncate.
precision	Utf8	The precision to truncate to.
Return Value

Returns a Duration truncated to the specified precision.

Examples
$ Truncate duration to year
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {duration::trunc(cast('P1Y2M3DT4H5M6S', duration), 'year')}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
duration::format

identity module
identity::id

Get the unique identifier of a record.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
identity::id()
Parameters

This function takes no parameters. It operates on the current record context.

Return Value

Returns a Utf8 containing the unique identifier of the current record.

Examples
$ Get the record identifier
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {identity::id()}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run

meta::type

is Module

Type checking and value inspection functions.

Value Checks
is::some

Check if a value is not none (has some value).

is::none

Check if a value is none (has no value).

Type Checks
is::type

Check if a value is of a specified type.

Permission Checks
is::root

Check if the current user has root privileges.

is::anonymous

Check if the current user is anonymous (not authenticated).

Function Reference
Function	Description
is::some	Check if a value is not none (has some value)
is::none	Check if a value is none (has no value)
is::type	Check if a value is of a specified type
is::root	Check if the current user has root privileges
is::anonymous	Check if the current user is anonymous (not authenticated)

is module
is::none

Check if a value is none (has no value).

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
is::none(value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Any	The value to check
Return Value

Returns a Boolean indicating whether the value is none.

Examples
$ Check if a value is none
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {is::none(cast(42, int4))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run

is::type

is module
is::some

Check if a value is not none (has some value).

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
is::some(value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Any	The value to check
Return Value

Returns a Boolean indicating whether the value is not none.

Examples
$ Check if a value is some
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {is::some(cast(42, int4))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
is::none
is::type

is module
is::type

Check if a value is of a specified type.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
is::type(value, type_name)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Any	The value to check the type of
type_name	Utf8	The name of the type to check against
Return Value

Returns a Boolean indicating whether the value is of the specified type.

Examples
$ Check if a value is a specific type
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {is::type(cast(42, int4), int4)}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
is::some
meta::type

json Module

Build and serialize JSON values directly in your queries.

Quick Example
$ Quick Example
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
3
from app::users
extend { payload: json::object("name", name, "age", age) }
map { name, payload }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Available Functions
json::array

Build a JSON array from a list of values

json::object

Build a JSON object from key-value pairs

json::serialize

Serialize a JSON value to a compact string

json::pretty

Serialize a JSON value to a pretty-printed string

Function Reference
Function	Description
json::array	Build a JSON array from a list of values
json::object	Build a JSON object from key-value pairs
json::serialize	Serialize a JSON value to a compact string
json::pretty	Serialize a JSON value to a pretty-printed string

json module · Function
json::array

Build a JSON array from a list of values.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
json::array(value1, value2, ...)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value1, value2, ...	Any	Values to include in the array
Return Value

Returns a JSON array containing the provided values.

Examples
Build a JSON array
$ Build JSON Array
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
3
from app::users
extend { tags: json::array("user", name, age) }
map { name, tags }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Mixed types
$ Inline JSON Array
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
from [{ label: "test" }]
extend { items: json::array(1, "two", true, none) }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
json::object
json::serialize
json::pretty

json module · Function
json::object

Build a JSON object from alternating key-value pairs.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
json::object(key1, value1, key2, value2, ...)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
key1, key2, ...	utf8	Field names for the object
value1, value2, ...	Any	Values for each field
Return Value

Returns a JSON object with the specified key-value pairs.

Examples
Build a JSON object
$ Build JSON Object
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
3
from app::users
extend { profile: json::object("name", name, "status", status) }
map { name, profile }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Nested objects
$ Nested JSON Structure
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
3
from app::users
extend { data: json::object("user", json::object("name", name, "age", age)) }
map { name, data }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
json::array
json::serialize
json::pretty

json module · Function
json::pretty

Serialize a JSON value to a pretty-printed string with indentation.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
json::pretty(value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	JSON	A JSON value (from json::array or json::object)
Return Value

Returns a utf8 string containing the indented JSON representation.

Examples
Pretty-print JSON
$ Pretty-print JSON
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
3
from app::users
extend { formatted: json::pretty(json::object("name", name, "age", age)) }
map { name, formatted }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
json::serialize
json::array
json::object

json module · Function
json::serialize

Serialize a JSON value to a compact string.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
json::serialize(value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	JSON	A JSON value (from json::array or json::object)
Return Value

Returns a utf8 string containing the compact JSON representation.

Examples
Serialize to string
$ Serialize to JSON String
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
3
from app::users
extend { json_str: json::serialize(json::object("name", name, "age", age)) }
map { name, json_str }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
json::pretty
json::array
json::object

math Module

Number crunching and aggregations for your queries.

Quick Example
$ Quick Example
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
3
4
5
from app::sales
aggregate {
  total_revenue: math::sum(amount),
  avg_sale: math::avg(amount)
} by {region}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Aggregation Functions

Use these with the aggregate transform to summarize your data.

math::sum

Calculate the sum of numeric values

math::avg

Calculate the average of numeric values

math::min

Find the minimum value

math::max

Find the maximum value

Scalar Functions

Apply math to individual values, row by row.

math::abs

Get the absolute value of a number

math::round

Round to the nearest integer or decimal place

math::floor

Round down to the nearest integer

math::ceil

Round up to the nearest integer

math::power

Raise a number to a power

math::sqrt

Compute the square root

math::sign

Return the sign of a number (-1, 0, or 1)

math::clamp

Clamp a value within a range

math::mod

Compute the modulo (remainder)

math::truncate

Truncate a number toward zero

math::gcd

Greatest common divisor

math::lcm

Least common multiple

Trigonometric Functions

Standard trigonometric functions operating in radians.

math::sin

Compute the sine

math::cos

Compute the cosine

math::tan

Compute the tangent

math::asin

Compute the arcsine

math::acos

Compute the arccosine

math::atan

Compute the arctangent

math::atan2

Two-argument arctangent

Logarithmic & Exponential

Logarithms, exponentials, and mathematical constants.

math::exp

Compute e raised to a power

math::log

Natural logarithm (base e)

math::log2

Base-2 logarithm

math::log10

Base-10 logarithm

math::e

Euler's number (~2.71828)

math::pi

Pi (~3.14159)

Function Reference
Function	Type	Description
math::sum	Aggregation	Calculate the sum of numeric values
math::avg	Aggregation	Calculate the average of numeric values
math::min	Aggregation	Find the minimum value
math::max	Aggregation	Find the maximum value
math::abs	Scalar	Get the absolute value of a number
math::round	Scalar	Round to the nearest integer or decimal place
math::floor	Scalar	Round down to the nearest integer
math::ceil	Scalar	Round up to the nearest integer
math::power	Scalar	Raise a number to a power
math::sqrt	Scalar	Compute the square root
math::sign	Scalar	Return the sign of a number
math::clamp	Scalar	Clamp a value within a range
math::mod	Scalar	Compute the modulo (remainder)
math::truncate	Scalar	Truncate a number toward zero
math::gcd	Scalar	Greatest common divisor
math::lcm	Scalar	Least common multiple
math::sin	Trigonometric	Compute the sine
math::cos	Trigonometric	Compute the cosine
math::tan	Trigonometric	Compute the tangent
math::asin	Trigonometric	Compute the arcsine
math::acos	Trigonometric	Compute the arccosine
math::atan	Trigonometric	Compute the arctangent
math::atan2	Trigonometric	Two-argument arctangent
math::exp	Exponential	Compute e raised to a power
math::log	Logarithmic	Natural logarithm (base e)
math::log2	Logarithmic	Base-2 logarithm
math::log10	Logarithmic	Base-10 logarithm
math::e	Constant	Euler's number (~2.71828)
math::pi	Constant	Pi (~3.14159)

math module · Scalar Function
math::abs

Get the absolute value of a number.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
math::abs(value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Number	The number to get the absolute value of
Return Value

Returns the absolute value of the input. Negative numbers become positive, positive numbers remain unchanged.

Examples
Convert to absolute value
$ Convert to absolute value
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
from app::transactions
extend { abs_amount: math::abs(amount) }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Filter by absolute magnitude
$ Filter by absolute magnitude
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
from app::balances
filter { math::abs(balance) > 1000 }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
math::round
math::power

math module · Scalar Function
math::acos

Compute the arccosine (inverse cosine) of a value in radians.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
math::acos(value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Number	A number between -1 and 1
Return Value

Returns a Number representing the arccosine in radians.

Examples
$ Calculate arccosine
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {math::acos(cast(0.5, float8))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
math::cos
math::asin
math::atan

math module · Scalar Function
math::asin

Compute the arcsine (inverse sine) of a value in radians.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
math::asin(value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Number	A number between -1 and 1
Return Value

Returns a Number representing the arcsine in radians.

Examples
$ Calculate arcsine
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {math::asin(cast(0.5, float8))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run

math module · Scalar Function
math::atan

Compute the arctangent (inverse tangent) of a value in radians.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
math::atan(value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Number	The value to compute the arctangent of
Return Value

Returns a Number representing the arctangent in radians.

Examples
$ Calculate arctangent
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {math::atan(cast(1.0, float8))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run

math module · Scalar Function
math::atan2

Compute the two-argument arctangent, returning the angle in radians between the positive x-axis and the point (x, y).

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
math::atan2(y, x)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
y	Number	The y-coordinate
x	Number	The x-coordinate
Return Value

Returns a Number representing the angle in radians.

Examples
$ Calculate two-argument arctangent
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {math::atan2(cast(1.0, float8), cast(1.0, float8))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
math::atan
math::cos
math::sin

math module · Aggregation Function
math::avg

Calculate the average (mean) of numeric values across multiple rows.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
math::avg(value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Number	The numeric field to average
Return Value

Returns the arithmetic mean of all values in the group.

Examples
Calculate average price
$ Calculate average price
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
from app::products
aggregate {math::avg(price)} by {category}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Average with total
$ Average with total
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
3
4
5
from app::sales
aggregate {
  avg_sale: math::avg(amount),
  total_sales: math::sum(amount)
}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Info
This is an aggregation function. Use it with the aggregate transform to compute values across multiple rows.
Related Functions
math::sum
math::min
math::max

math module · Scalar Function
math::ceil

Round up to the nearest integer.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
math::ceil(value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Number	The number to round up
Return Value

Returns the smallest integer greater than or equal to the input value.

Examples
$ Ceil a float
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {math::ceil(cast(3.2, float8))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
math::floor
math::round

math module · Scalar Function
math::clamp

Clamp a value within a specified range.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
math::clamp(value, min, max)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Number	The value to clamp
min	Number	The minimum bound
max	Number	The maximum bound
Return Value

Returns a Number clamped within the specified range.

Examples
$ Clamp value to range
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {math::clamp(cast(5, int4), cast(0, int4), cast(10, int4))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run

math module · Scalar Function
math::cos

Compute the cosine of an angle in radians.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
math::cos(angle)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
angle	Number	The angle in radians
Return Value

Returns a Number representing the cosine of the angle.

Examples
$ Calculate cosine
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {math::cos(cast(0.0, float8))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run

math module · Scalar Function
math::e

Return Euler's number (approximately 2.71828).

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
math::e()
Parameters

This function takes no parameters.

Return Value

Returns a Number representing Euler's number (approximately 2.71828).

Examples
$ Get Euler number
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {math::e()}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run

math module · Scalar Function
math::exp

Compute Euler's number (e) raised to the given power.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
math::exp(value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Number	The exponent to raise e to
Return Value

Returns a Number representing e raised to the given power.

Examples
$ Calculate exponential
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {math::exp(cast(1.0, float8))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run

math module · Scalar Function
math::floor

Round down to the nearest integer.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
math::floor(value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Number	The number to round down
Return Value

Returns the largest integer less than or equal to the input value.

Examples
$ Floor a float
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {math::floor(cast(3.7, float8))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
math::ceil
math::round

math module · Scalar Function
math::gcd

Compute the greatest common divisor of two integers.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
math::gcd(a, b)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
a	Integer	The first integer
b	Integer	The second integer
Return Value

Returns an Integer representing the greatest common divisor.

Examples
$ Greatest common divisor
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {math::gcd(cast(12, int4), cast(8, int4))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
math::lcm
math::mod
PRODUCT

math module · Scalar Function
math::lcm

Compute the least common multiple of two integers.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
math::lcm(a, b)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
a	Integer	The first integer
b	Integer	The second integer
Return Value

Returns an Integer representing the least common multiple.

Examples
$ Least common multiple
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {math::lcm(cast(4, int4), cast(6, int4))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
math::gcd
math::mod

math module · Scalar Function
math::log

Compute the natural logarithm (base e) of a value.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
math::log(value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Number	A positive number
Return Value

Returns a Number representing the natural logarithm of the value.

Examples
$ Calculate natural logarithm
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {math::log(cast(2.0, float8))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
math::log2
math::log10
math::exp

math module · Scalar Function
math::log10

Compute the base-10 logarithm of a value.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
math::log10(value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Number	A positive number
Return Value

Returns a Number representing the base-10 logarithm of the value.

Examples
$ Calculate base-10 logarithm
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {math::log10(cast(100.0, float8))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
math::log
math::log2

math module · Scalar Function
math::log2

Compute the base-2 logarithm of a value.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
math::log2(value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Number	A positive number
Return Value

Returns a Number representing the base-2 logarithm of the value.

Examples
$ Calculate base-2 logarithm
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {math::log2(cast(8.0, float8))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run

math module · Aggregation Function
math::max

Find the maximum value across multiple rows.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
math::max(value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Number	The numeric field to find the maximum of
Return Value

Returns the largest value in the group.

Examples
Find highest price
$ Find highest price
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
from app::products
aggregate {math::max(price)} by {category}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Min and max together
$ Min and max together
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
3
4
5
from app::sales
aggregate {
  lowest: math::min(amount),
  highest: math::max(amount)
} by {month}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Info
This is an aggregation function. Use it with the aggregate transform to compute values across multiple rows.
Related Functions
math::min
math::avg
math::sum

math module · Aggregation Function
math::min

Find the minimum value across multiple rows.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
math::min(value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Number	The numeric field to find the minimum of
Return Value

Returns the smallest value in the group.

Examples
Find lowest price
$ Find lowest price
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
from app::products
aggregate {math::min(price)} by {category}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Min and max together
$ Min and max together
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
3
4
5
from app::sales
aggregate {
  lowest: math::min(amount),
  highest: math::max(amount)
} by {month}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Info
This is an aggregation function. Use it with the aggregate transform to compute values across multiple rows.
Related Functions
math::max
math::avg
math::sum

math module · Scalar Function
math::mod

Compute the modulo (remainder) of a division.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
math::mod(a, b)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
a	Number	The dividend
b	Number	The divisor
Return Value

Returns a Number representing the remainder of the division.

Examples
$ Modulo operation
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {math::mod(cast(7, int4), cast(3, int4))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
math::gcd
math::truncate

math module · Scalar Function
math::pi

Return the mathematical constant pi (approximately 3.14159).

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
math::pi()
Parameters

This function takes no parameters.

Return Value

Returns a Number representing the mathematical constant pi (approximately 3.14159).

Examples
$ Get Pi constant
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {math::pi()}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run

math module · Scalar Function
math::power

Raise a number to a power (exponentiation).

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
math::power(base, exponent)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
base	Number	The base number
exponent	Number	The power to raise the base to
Return Value

Returns the result of raising the base to the specified exponent (base^exponent).

Examples
$ Calculate power
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {math::power(cast(2, int4), cast(3, int4))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
math::abs
math::round

math module · Scalar Function
math::round

Round to the nearest integer or decimal place.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
math::round(value, decimal_places)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Number	The number to round
decimal_places	Integer	Number of decimal places to round to (optional)
Return Value

Returns the number rounded to the specified number of decimal places. If decimal_places is omitted, rounds to the nearest integer.

Examples
Round to 2 decimal places
$ Round to 2 decimal places
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
from app::prices
extend { rounded: math::round(price, 2) }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Round to nearest integer
$ Round to nearest integer
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
from app::metrics
extend { rounded_value: math::round(value) }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
math::floor
math::ceil
math::abs

math module · Scalar Function
math::sign

Return the sign of a number (-1, 0, or 1).

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
math::sign(value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Number	The number to determine the sign of
Return Value

Returns an Integer: -1 for negative numbers, 0 for zero, or 1 for positive numbers.

Examples
$ Get sign of number
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {math::sign(cast(-50000, int4))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run

math::abs

math module · Scalar Function
math::sin

Compute the sine of an angle in radians.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
math::sin(angle)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
angle	Number	The angle in radians
Return Value

Returns a Number representing the sine of the angle.

Examples
$ Calculate sine
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {math::sin(cast(1.0, float8))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run

math module · Scalar Function
math::sqrt

Compute the square root of a value.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
math::sqrt(value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Number	A non-negative number
Return Value

Returns a Number representing the square root of the value.

Examples
$ Calculate square root
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {math::sqrt(cast(16.0, float8))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
math::power
math::abs

math module · Aggregation Function
math::sum

Calculate the sum of numeric values across multiple rows.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
math::sum(value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Number	The numeric field to sum
Return Value

Returns the total sum of all values in the group.

Examples
Calculate total revenue
$ Calculate total revenue
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
from app::orders
aggregate {math::sum(total)} by {region}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Multiple aggregations
$ Multiple aggregations
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
3
4
5
from app::sales
aggregate {
  total_revenue: math::sum(amount),
  avg_sale: math::avg(amount)
}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Info
This is an aggregation function. Use it with the aggregate transform to compute values across multiple rows.
Related Functions
math::avg
math::min
math::max

math module · Scalar Function
math::truncate

Truncate a number toward zero.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
math::truncate(value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Number	The number to truncate
Return Value

Returns a Number truncated toward zero.

Examples
$ Truncate a float
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {math::truncate(cast(3.5, float8))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run

math::ceil

meta Module

Functions for inspecting value metadata and types.

Functions
meta::type

Get the type name of a value as a string.

Function Reference
Function	Description
meta::type	Get the type name of a value as a string

meta module
meta::type

Get the type name of a value as a string.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
meta::type(value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Any	The value to get the type name of
Return Value

Returns a Utf8 containing the type name of the value.

Examples
$ Get the type of a value
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {meta::type(cast(42, int4))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
is::type

text Module

String manipulation functions for cleaning, formatting, and transforming text in your queries.

Quick Example
$ Quick Example
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
3
4
5
6
from app::users
extend {
  full_name: text::concat(first_name, " ", last_name),
  email_lower: text::lower(email)
}
filter { text::length(full_name) > 0 }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Case & Whitespace
text::lower

Convert a string to lowercase

text::upper

Convert a string to uppercase

text::trim

Remove leading and trailing whitespace

text::trim_start

Remove leading whitespace

text::trim_end

Remove trailing whitespace

Search & Match
text::contains

Check if a string contains a substring

text::starts_with

Check if a string starts with a prefix

text::ends_with

Check if a string ends with a suffix

text::index_of

Find the index of a substring

text::count

Count the number of characters in a string

Manipulation
text::concat

Concatenate multiple strings together

text::substring

Extract a portion of a string

text::replace

Replace all occurrences of a substring

text::repeat

Repeat a string multiple times

text::reverse

Reverse the characters in a string

text::pad_left

Pad a string on the left

text::pad_right

Pad a string on the right

Measurement & Encoding
text::length

Get the length of a string

text::ascii

Get the ASCII code of the first character

text::char

Convert an ASCII code to a character

Formatting
text::format_bytes

Format bytes using binary units (KiB, MiB)

text::format_bytes_si

Format bytes using SI units (KB, MB)

Function Reference
Function	Description
text::ascii	Get the ASCII code of the first character
text::char	Convert an ASCII code to a character
text::concat	Concatenate multiple strings together
text::contains	Check if a string contains a substring
text::count	Count the number of characters in a string
text::ends_with	Check if a string ends with a suffix
text::format_bytes	Format bytes using binary units (KiB, MiB)
text::format_bytes_si	Format bytes using SI units (KB, MB)
text::index_of	Find the index of a substring
text::length	Get the length of a string
text::lower	Convert a string to lowercase
text::pad_left	Pad a string on the left
text::pad_right	Pad a string on the right
text::repeat	Repeat a string multiple times
text::replace	Replace all occurrences of a substring
text::reverse	Reverse the characters in a string
text::starts_with	Check if a string starts with a prefix
text::substring	Extract a portion of a string
text::trim	Remove leading and trailing whitespace
text::trim_end	Remove trailing whitespace
text::trim_start	Remove leading whitespace
text::upper	Convert a string to uppercase

text module
text::ascii

Get the ASCII code of the first character in a string.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
text::ascii(value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Utf8	The string whose first character's ASCII code will be returned.
Return Value

Returns an Integer representing the ASCII code of the first character in the string.

Examples
$ Get ASCII code
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {text::ascii('A')}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
text::char

text module
text::char

Convert an ASCII code to its corresponding character.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
text::char(code)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
code	Integer	The ASCII code to convert to a character.
Return Value

Returns a Utf8 containing the single character corresponding to the given ASCII code.

Examples
$ Get character from code
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {text::char(65)}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
text::ascii

text module
text::concat

Concatenate multiple strings together.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
text::concat(value1, value2, ...)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value1, value2, ...	Utf8	Two or more strings to concatenate together
Return Value

Returns a new string with all input strings joined together in order.

Examples
$ Concatenate two strings
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {text::concat('hello', ' world')}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
text::substring
text::length

text module
text::contains

Check if a string contains a specified substring.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
text::contains(value, search)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Utf8	The string to search within.
search	Utf8	The substring to search for.
Return Value

Returns a Boolean indicating whether the string contains the specified substring.

Examples
$ Check if string contains substring
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {text::contains('hello world', 'lo wo')}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
text::starts_with
text::ends_with
text::index_of

text module
text::count

Count the number of characters in a string.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
text::count(value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Utf8	The string to count characters in
Return Value

Returns an Integer representing the number of characters in the string.

Examples
$ Count characters in string
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {text::count('hello')}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
text::contains
text::length

text module
text::ends_with

Check if a string ends with a specified suffix.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
text::ends_with(value, suffix)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Utf8	The string to check.
suffix	Utf8	The suffix to check for.
Return Value

Returns a Boolean indicating whether the string ends with the specified suffix.

Examples
$ Check if string ends with suffix
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {text::ends_with('hello world', 'world')}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
text::starts_with
text::contains

text module
text::format_bytes

Format a byte count as a human-readable string using binary units (KiB, MiB, GiB).

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
text::format_bytes(bytes)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
bytes	Integer	The byte count to format.
Return Value

Returns a Utf8 representing the byte count in human-readable binary units (KiB, MiB, GiB).

Examples
$ Format bytes (binary)
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {text::format_bytes(cast(1048576, int4))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
text::format_bytes_si

text module
text::format_bytes_si

Format a byte count as a human-readable string using SI units (KB, MB, GB).

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
text::format_bytes_si(bytes)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
bytes	Integer	The byte count to format.
Return Value

Returns a Utf8 representing the byte count in human-readable SI units (KB, MB, GB).

Examples
$ Format bytes (SI)
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {text::format_bytes_si(cast(1000000, int4))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
text::format_bytes

text module
text::index_of

Find the index of the first occurrence of a substring.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
text::index_of(value, search)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Utf8	The string to search within.
search	Utf8	The substring to find.
Return Value

Returns an Integer representing the 0-based index of the first occurrence of the substring, or -1 if not found.

Examples
$ Find index of substring
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {text::index_of('hello world', 'world')}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
text::contains
text::substring

text module
text::length

Get the length of a string.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
text::length(value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Utf8	The string to measure
Return Value

Returns an integer representing the number of characters in the string.

Examples
Filter by minimum length
$ Filter by minimum length
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
from app::posts
filter { text::length(content) > 100 }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Add character count field
$ Add character count field
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
from app::articles
extend { char_count: text::length(body) }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Validate input length
$ Validate input length
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
from app::usernames
filter { text::length(username) >= 3 and text::length(username) <= 20 }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
text::substring
text::trim

text module
text::lower

Convert a string to lowercase.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
text::lower(value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Utf8	The string to convert to lowercase
Return Value

Returns a new string with all characters converted to lowercase.

Examples
$ Convert to lowercase
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {text::lower('HELLO')}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
text::upper
text::trim

text module
text::pad_left

Pad a string on the left to a specified length.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
text::pad_left(value, length, pad_char)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Utf8	The string to pad.
length	Integer	The desired total length of the resulting string.
pad_char	Utf8	The character to use for padding.
Return Value

Returns a Utf8 padded on the left to the specified length.

Examples
$ Pad string on the left
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {text::pad_left('hi', 5, '0')}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
text::pad_right
text::length

text module
text::pad_right

Pad a string on the right to a specified length.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
text::pad_right(value, length, pad_char)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Utf8	The string to pad.
length	Integer	The desired total length of the resulting string.
pad_char	Utf8	The character to use for padding.
Return Value

Returns a Utf8 padded on the right to the specified length.

Examples
$ Pad string on the right
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {text::pad_right('hi', 5, '0')}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
text::pad_left
text::length

text module
text::repeat

Repeat a string a specified number of times.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
text::repeat(value, count)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Utf8	The string to repeat.
count	Integer	The number of times to repeat the string.
Return Value

Returns a Utf8 containing the original string repeated the specified number of times.

Examples
$ Repeat a string
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {text::repeat('ab', 3)}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run

text::concat
text::length

text module
text::replace

Replace all occurrences of a substring with another string.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
text::replace(value, search, replacement)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Utf8	The original string.
search	Utf8	The substring to search for.
replacement	Utf8	The string to replace each occurrence with.
Return Value

Returns a Utf8 with all occurrences of the search substring replaced by the replacement string.

Examples
$ Replace substring
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {text::replace('hello world', 'world', 'there')}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
text::contains
text::count

text module
text::reverse

Reverse the characters in a string.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
text::reverse(value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Utf8	The string to reverse.
Return Value

Returns a Utf8 with the characters in reverse order.

Examples
$ Reverse a string
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {text::reverse('hello')}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run

text module
text::starts_with

Check if a string starts with a specified prefix.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
text::starts_with(value, prefix)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Utf8	The string to check.
prefix	Utf8	The prefix to check for.
Return Value

Returns a Boolean indicating whether the string starts with the specified prefix.

Examples
$ Check if string starts with prefix
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {text::starts_with('hello world', 'hello')}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
text::ends_with
text::contains

text module
text::substring

Extract a portion of a string.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
text::substring(value, start, end)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Utf8	The string to extract from
start	Integer	The starting index (0-based)
end	Integer	The ending index (exclusive)
Return Value

Returns a new string containing the characters from the start index up to (but not including) the end index.

Examples
Extract prefix
$ Extract prefix
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
from app::codes
extend { prefix: text::substring(code, 0, 3) }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Get first characters
$ Get first characters
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
from app::products
extend { initial: text::substring(name, 0, 1) }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Extract middle portion
$ Extract middle portion
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
from app::identifiers
extend { middle: text::substring(code, 3, 7) }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
text::length
text::concat
text::trim

text module
text::trim

Remove leading and trailing whitespace from a string.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
text::trim(value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Utf8	The string to trim
Return Value

Returns a new string with leading and trailing whitespace removed.

Examples
Clean user input
$ Clean user input
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
from app::inputs
extend { clean_value: text::trim(value) }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Filter non-empty strings
$ Filter non-empty strings
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
from app::inputs
filter { text::length(value) > 0 }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
text::length
text::substring

text module
text::trim_end

Remove trailing whitespace from a string.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
text::trim_end(value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Utf8	The string to trim trailing whitespace from.
Return Value

Returns a Utf8 with trailing whitespace removed.

Examples
$ Trim trailing whitespace
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {text::trim_end('   hello   ')}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
text::trim
text::trim_start

text module
text::upper

Convert a string to uppercase.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
text::upper(value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
value	Utf8	The string to convert to uppercase
Return Value

Returns a new string with all characters converted to uppercase.

Examples
Format display names
$ Format display names
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
from app::users
extend { upper_name: text::upper(name) }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Uppercase code identifiers
$ Uppercase code identifiers
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
from app::products
extend { sku_upper: text::upper(sku) }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
text::lower
text::trim

time Module

Functions for working with time-of-day values, including extraction, construction, arithmetic, and formatting.

Extraction
time::hour

Extract the hour (0-23) from a time value.

time::minute

Extract the minute (0-59) from a time value.

time::second

Extract the second (0-59) from a time value.

time::nanosecond

Extract the nanosecond component from a time value.

Construction
time::new

Create a new time from hour, minute, and second components.

time::now

Get the current time of day.

Manipulation
time::add

Add a duration to a time value.

time::subtract

Subtract a duration from a time value.

time::diff

Calculate the difference between two time values.

time::trunc

Truncate a time to a specified precision.

time::age

Calculate the elapsed duration from a time value until now.

Formatting
time::format

Format a time value as a string.

Function Reference
Function	Description
time::hour	Extract the hour (0-23) from a time value.
time::minute	Extract the minute (0-59) from a time value.
time::second	Extract the second (0-59) from a time value.
time::nanosecond	Extract the nanosecond component from a time value.
time::new	Create a new time from hour, minute, and second components.
time::now	Get the current time of day.
time::add	Add a duration to a time value.
time::subtract	Subtract a duration from a time value.
time::diff	Calculate the difference between two time values.
time::trunc	Truncate a time to a specified precision.
time::age	Calculate the elapsed duration from a time value until now.
time::format	Format a time value as a string.

time module
time::add

Add a duration to a time value.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
time::add(time_value, duration)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
time_value	Time	The base time value
duration	Duration	The duration to add
Return Value

Returns a Time value representing the result of adding the duration to the time.

Examples
$ Add a duration to a time
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {time::add(cast('14:30:00', time), cast('PT1H', duration))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
time::subtract
time::diff

time module
time::age

Calculate the duration between two time values.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
time::age(time_a, time_b)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
time_a	Time	The first time value
time_b	Time	The second time value
Return Value

Returns a Duration representing the time between the two time values.

Examples
$ Calculate duration between two times
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {time::age(cast('14:00:00', time), cast('10:00:00', time))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
time::diff
time::now

time module
time::diff

Calculate the difference between two time values.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
time::diff(time_a, time_b)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
time_a	Time	The first time value
time_b	Time	The second time value
Return Value

Returns a Duration representing the difference between the two time values.

Examples
$ Calculate difference between two times
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {time::diff(cast('14:00:00', time), cast('10:00:00', time))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run

time::add
time::subtract

time module
time::format

Format a time value as a string.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
time::format(time_value, format_string)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
time_value	Time	The time value to format
format_string	Utf8	The format string specifying the output format
Return Value

Returns a Utf8 containing the formatted time representation.

Examples
$ Format a time as a string
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {time::format(cast('14:30:45', time), '%H:%M:%S')}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
time::trunc

time module
time::hour

Extract the hour (0-23) from a time value.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
time::hour(time_value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
time_value	Time	The time value to extract the hour from
Return Value

Returns an Integer representing the hour component (0-23) of the given time value.

Examples
$ Extract the hour
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {time::hour(cast('14:30:00', time))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
time::minute
time::second

time module
time::minute

Extract the minute (0-59) from a time value.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
time::minute(time_value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
time_value	Time	The time value to extract the minute from
Return Value

Returns an Integer representing the minute component (0-59) of the given time value.

Examples
$ Extract the minute
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {time::minute(cast('14:30:00', time))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run

time module
time::nanosecond

Extract the nanosecond component from a time value.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
time::nanosecond(time_value)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
time_value	Time	The time value to extract the nanosecond from
Return Value

Returns an Integer representing the nanosecond component of the given time value.

Examples
$ Extract nanoseconds
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {time::nanosecond(cast('14:30:45.123456789', time))}
$ ctrl+enter to run

time module
time::new

Create a new time from hour, minute, and second components.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
time::new(hour, minute, second)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
hour	Integer	The hour component (0-23)
minute	Integer	The minute component (0-59)
second	Integer	The second component (0-59)
Return Value

Returns a Time value constructed from the given hour, minute, and second components.

Examples
$ Create a new time
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {time::new(14, 30, 0)}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
time::now

time module
time::now

Get the current time of day.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
time::now()
Parameters

This function takes no parameters.

Return Value

Returns a Time value representing the current time of day.

Examples
$ Get the current time
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {time::now()}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run

datetime::now

time module
time::subtract

Subtract a duration from a time value.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
time::subtract(time_value, duration)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
time_value	Time	The base time value
duration	Duration	The duration to subtract
Return Value

Returns a Time value representing the result of subtracting the duration from the time.

Examples
$ Subtract a duration from a time
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {time::subtract(cast('14:30:00', time), cast('PT1H', duration))}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
time::add
time::diff

time module
time::trunc

Truncate a time to a specified precision.

Syntax
$ RQL
Copy
1
time::trunc(time_value, precision)
Parameters
Name	Type	Description
time_value	Time	The time value to truncate
precision	Utf8	The precision to truncate to
Return Value

Returns a Time value truncated to the specified precision.

Examples
$ Truncate to hour precision
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
map {time::trunc(cast('14:30:45', time), 'hour')}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Related Functions
time::format

Guide
Expire Data with TTL

Give rows a lifetime and let the engine drop them, instead of running cleanup jobs.

Sessions that clean themselves up

This guide will model a session table with with { row: { ttl: { duration: '1h', mode: drop } } } and show expiry flowing into dependent views.

Choosing durations and modes

This section will cover the available modes and how to pick TTLs that match your consistency needs.

Guide
Import & Export Data

Move data into and out of ReifyDB: bulk inserts, exports, and getting existing datasets loaded.

Bulk loading

INSERT takes arrays of records and the engine supports bulk paths for large loads. This guide will cover batching strategy and performance expectations.

Exporting

This section will document the export facilities and formats, based on the export examples that ship with the Rust SDK.

Guide
Build Incremental Views

Design derived state with transactional and deferred views, and know which to pick.

Transactional when reads must be exact

Balances, inventories, anything a subsequent read depends on. This guide will build one and demonstrate read-your-writes behavior.

Deferred when writes must be cheap

Dashboards, counters, windowed rollups. This guide will build one and show the propagation delay in practice.

Composing views

Views can read from views. This section will cover layering and the operational limits.

Model Application State

Take one realistic domain - a small task tracker - and model it end to end with ReifyDB's schema primitives: a namespace to own everything, tables for the entities, an enum for a closed value set, a dictionary for an open one, auto-increment sequences for IDs, a transactional view for derived state, and a ring buffer for recent activity. You end up with a working schema and the queries that drive it.

This page is a real database
Every snippet runs against a real ReifyDB engine compiled to WebAssembly. Click Run on each snippet, top to bottom - later snippets build on the state left by earlier ones. If you have not done the quickstart yet, start there.
1. A namespace and the first table

Everything the tracker owns lives in one namespace. One namespace per application domain is the right default: it keeps names short, and dropping or inspecting the whole app stays a one-liner. The first entity is projects. Its id column carries with { auto_increment }, so the database owns ID assignment - your application never coordinates counters:

$ Create the Namespace and the Projects Table
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
3
4
5
create namespace tracker;
create table tracker::projects {
  id: int8 with { auto_increment },
  name: utf8
}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run

Because the database assigns IDs, you read them back at insert time with returning instead of guessing:

$ Insert Projects and Read the Generated IDs
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
3
4
insert tracker::projects [
  { name: "Website" },
  { name: "Mobile App" }
] returning { id, name }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
2. A closed value set: enum

Task priority is a fixed set known at design time. That is exactly what an enum is for: the schema enforces the set, so a typo like "hgih" is a schema error instead of silent bad data. Declare the variants once:

$ Declare Priority as an Enum
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
create enum tracker::priority { Low, Medium, High }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
3. An open value set: dictionary

Assignee names are the opposite case: an open set you discover at runtime. A dictionary interns each distinct string once and stores a compact integer everywhere it is used. You still read and write plain strings; the encoding is the engine's problem:

$ Create a Dictionary for Assignees
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
create dictionary tracker::assignees for utf8 as uint2
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
4. The tasks table ties it together

Now the central entity. Each column type is a decision you just made: an auto-increment ID, a plain int8 foreign reference to projects, the enum for priority, and the dictionary-encoded assignee:

$ The Tasks Table Ties It Together
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
3
4
5
6
7
8
create table tracker::tasks {
  id: int8 with { auto_increment },
  project_id: int8,
  title: utf8,
  priority: tracker::priority,
  assignee: utf8 with { dictionary: tracker::assignees },
  done: bool
}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
5. Derived state is a view, not a cache

"How many open tasks does each person have?" is the kind of read shape that usually ends up in a cache with invalidation bugs. Here it is a transactional view: declared once, maintained incrementally inside the same transaction as every write that affects it. Define it before inserting data, so it tracks changes from the start:

$ Derived State: Open Tasks per Assignee
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
3
4
5
create transactional view tracker::workload { assignee: utf8, open: int8 } as {
  from tracker::tasks
  filter { done == false }
  aggregate { open: math::count(id) } by { assignee }
}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
6. Recent activity is a ring buffer

An activity feed only ever shows the last few entries, so storing unbounded history is waste. A ring buffer holds a fixed number of rows and evicts the oldest on overflow - retention is schema, not a cleanup job. The tiny capacity here is just to make eviction visible on this page:

$ A Ring Buffer for Recent Activity
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
3
4
create ringbuffer tracker::activity {
  task_id: int8,
  action: utf8
} with { capacity: 3 }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
7. Load the data

The schema is complete; now exercise it. Enum values are written as variant paths, and assignees are written as plain strings - the dictionary interns new names on the fly:

$ Insert Tasks
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
3
4
5
6
7
8
insert tracker::tasks [
  { project_id: 1, title: "Design landing page", priority: tracker::priority::High,
    assignee: "ada", done: false },
  { project_id: 1, title: "Set up analytics", priority: tracker::priority::Low,
    assignee: "grace", done: false },
  { project_id: 2, title: "Login screen", priority: tracker::priority::Medium,
    assignee: "ada", done: false }
]
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run

Every auto-increment column is backed by a sequence you can reposition, for example to keep public-facing IDs away from the small numbers used while prototyping. The next insert continues from the new value:

$ Reposition the ID Sequence
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
3
4
5
alter sequence tracker::tasks::id set value 100;
insert tracker::tasks [
  { project_id: 2, title: "Push notifications", priority: tracker::priority::Low,
    assignee: "grace", done: false }
] returning { id, title }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
8. Read it back

A plain read shows how each choice is stored. The enum column comes back as its compact tag (priority_tag: declaration order, so Low is 0 and High is 2), while the dictionary-encoded assignee is decoded back to a string for you:

$ Read the Tasks Back
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
from tracker::tasks
sort { id: asc }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run

The dictionary itself is queryable, and it interned exactly the two distinct names it saw:

$ The Dictionary Interned Both Assignees
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
from tracker::assignees
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
9. Query the derived state

The workload view is already populated. Nothing recomputed it - the task inserts maintained it as they committed:

$ Query the Workload View
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
from tracker::workload
sort { assignee: asc }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run

Ad-hoc read shapes stay ordinary queries. Joined columns are prefixed with the join alias, so the project's name arrives as p_name:

$ Join Tasks to Their Project
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
3
4
5
from tracker::tasks
filter { done == false }
inner join { from tracker::projects } as p using (project_id, p.id)
map { title, project: p_name }
sort { title: asc }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
10. Change the data

Complete a task and record the action in the activity buffer:

$ Complete a Task and Log the Activity
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
update tracker::tasks { done: true } filter { title == "Login screen" };
insert tracker::activity [{ task_id: 3, action: "completed" }]
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run

The completed task no longer matches the view's filter, so its assignee's count dropped by one. There is no cache to invalidate, because there is no cache:

$ The View Already Reflects the Change
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
from tracker::workload
sort { assignee: asc }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run

Meanwhile the activity buffer enforces its own retention. It held one row; three more inserts push it past its capacity of 3, and the oldest entry is evicted:

$ The Ring Buffer Keeps Only the Newest Rows
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
3
4
5
6
insert tracker::activity [
  { task_id: 1, action: "commented" },
  { task_id: 2, action: "reassigned" },
  { task_id: 1, action: "completed" }
];
from tracker::activity
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
What you modeled
--
Authoritative state: two tables with database-assigned IDs.
--
Constrained values: an enum for the closed set, a dictionary for the open one - both enforced or encoded by the schema, not by convention.
--
Derived state: a transactional view that every write keeps current.
--
Bounded history: a ring buffer whose retention is part of the schema.
Where next
--
The data model - every primitive used here, in reference depth
--
Transactions - why the view and the write can never disagree
--
RQL in five minutes - the query language behind every snippet on this page

Guide
Windowed Aggregation over Live Data

Maintain per-interval rollups (counts, sums, rates) with window inside a deferred view.

Tumbling windows

window tumbling { ... } with { interval: '5s' } buckets rows by time. This guide will build a live request-rate rollup end to end.

Reading windowed results

Querying the view returns one row per bucket. This section will cover bucket boundaries and late data.

Installation

Get ReifyDB on your machine. It takes about two minutes.

Requirements
•
Rust 1.90+ - ReifyDB is written in Rust.
•
Cargo - Rust's package manager (included with Rust).
Install Rust

If you do not have Rust installed yet, grab it with rustup:

BASH
Copy
curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://sh.rustup.rs | sh

Follow the prompts to complete the installation, then restart your terminal.

Install ReifyDB

Once Rust is ready, install ReifyDB with Cargo:

BASH
Copy
cargo install reifydb
Build from Source

If you prefer building from source:

BASH
Copy
git clone https://github.com/reifydb/reifydb.git
cd reifydb
cargo build --release

The binary will be available at target/release/reifydb.

Verify Installation

Make sure everything worked:

BASH
Copy
reifydb --version
Next Steps
You are all set. Head to the Quick Start guide and write your first query.

Operate
Storage & Configuration

Configure the engine: storage backend, logging, and optional subsystems.

Storage backends

In-memory or SQLite-backed persistence. This section will document the configuration for each and how to size them.

Environment and logging

Log levels and tracing are controlled via environment variables. This section will list the supported settings.

Optional subsystems

Metrics, tracing, and other subsystems are feature-gated. This section will describe what each adds and its cost.

Operate
Metrics & Observability

The engine's own state is queryable with RQL through system vtables, and exportable via OpenTelemetry.

System vtables

Storage and engine metrics are ordinary queryable sources:

RQL
Copy
from system::metrics::storage::table::current

This section will catalog the available vtables.

OpenTelemetry

The server can export traces and metrics via OTEL. This section will cover enabling and configuring the exporter.

Operate
Run the Server

Start reifydb-server, know its ports, and shut it down cleanly.

Starting
BASH
Copy
reifydb-server

By default the server listens for HTTP on 0.0.0.0:8090 and WebSocket on 0.0.0.0:8091, with admin endpoints bound to localhost. This section will document every port and flag.

Lifecycle

The server runs until signalled and shuts down gracefully. This section will cover signals, draining, and what happens to unflushed state.

Docker

This section will provide a container image reference and a minimal docker run example.

Quickstart

Create a table, define a live view over it, and watch ReifyDB keep that view up to date as the data changes. Right here in your browser, nothing to install.

This page is a real database
Every snippet below runs against a real ReifyDB engine compiled to WebAssembly. Click Run on each snippet, top to bottom. You can edit any of them.
1. Create a table

Tables hold authoritative state. Every table lives in a namespace and is addressed as namespace::table:

$ Create a Table
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
create namespace shop;
create table shop::orders { id: int4, item: utf8, amount: float8, status: utf8 }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
2. Define a live view

A transactional view is derived state. You declare the query once; ReifyDB maintains the result incrementally, inside the same transaction as every write that affects it. No polling, no cache invalidation, no batch refresh:

$ Create a Transactional View
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
3
4
5
create transactional view shop::open_orders { id: int4, item: utf8, amount: float8 } as {
  from shop::orders
  filter { status == "open" }
  map { id, item, amount }
}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run

The view tracks changes from the moment it is created, so we define it before inserting data.

3. Insert rows

INSERT takes an array of records:

$ Insert Rows
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
3
4
5
6
INSERT shop::orders [
  { id: 1, item: "keyboard", amount: 89.0, status: "open" },
  { id: 2, item: "monitor", amount: 349.5, status: "open" },
  { id: 3, item: "cable", amount: 12.5, status: "shipped" },
  { id: 4, item: "desk", amount: 420.0, status: "shipped" }
]
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
4. Query the table

RQL queries are pipelines. Start with from, then chain steps; each line transforms the output of the line above it:

$ Query the Table
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
3
from shop::orders
filter { status == "open" }
sort { id: asc }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
5. Query the view

The view already contains the two open orders. Nothing recomputed the query; the inserts themselves maintained it:

$ Query the View
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
from shop::open_orders
sort { id: asc }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
6. Change the data

Ship the keyboard order:

$ Update a Row
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
UPDATE shop::orders { status: "shipped" } FILTER { id == 1 }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run

Then query the view again. The shipped order is gone, because the view's filter no longer matches it:

$ The View Maintained Itself
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
from shop::open_orders
sort { id: asc }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
7. Aggregate

Pipelines end wherever you need them to. Group and aggregate with aggregate ... by:

$ Aggregate Revenue by Status
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
3
from shop::orders
aggregate { revenue: math::sum(amount), orders: math::count(id) } by { status }
sort { status: asc }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run
Where next
--

--

Resources
For AI Agents

Machine-readable entry points for coding assistants working with ReifyDB and RQL.

llms.txt

A curated index of these docs lives at /llms.txt, and the full text of every page is concatenated at /llms-full.txt:

BASH
Copy
curl https://reifydb.com/llms.txt
curl https://reifydb.com/llms-full.txt
Ground rules for generated RQL

RQL is not SQL: there is no SELECT, projection is map, filtering is filter, grouping is aggregate ... by, and the missing value is none, never null. Pointing an agent at the RQL section of these docs prevents most invented syntax.

Resources
Caveats & Limitations

Known sharp edges, stated plainly. ReifyDB is in development and not yet recommended for production.

Flow transactions and read sets

Flow transactions do not currently track read sets, which is safe only for flows over disjoint keys. This section will keep the authoritative list of concurrency caveats in sync with the engine.

Durability window

Persistence is asynchronous by design; a crash can lose the most recent commits. This section will quantify the window per storage backend.

Pre-1.0 stability

Syntax and APIs can still change between releases. Breaking changes are called out in release notes.

Resources
Troubleshooting & FAQ

Common errors, what they mean, and answers to the questions everyone asks first.

Common errors

RQL errors carry stable codes (AST_*, RUNTIME_*, FUNCTION_*) with location and help text. This section will index the frequent ones with fixes.

Frequently asked questions

Is RQL a SQL dialect? (No.) Can I use ReifyDB as my only database? When do views recompute? This section will collect and answer real questions as they come in.

Resources
Roadmap & Experimental

ReifyDB is pre-1.0 and in active development. These docs describe what works today; everything below exists in the codebase but is not yet stable enough to document.

Experimental features
Feature	Status
Events, handlers, and dispatch	Implemented in the engine, API still settling.
User-defined functions and WASM extensions	Implemented, interface not yet stable.
Sources, sinks, and bindings (HTTP, gRPC, WS)	In development.
Raft replication and consensus	In development.
Wallet-based authentication	Experimental.
Deterministic simulation client	Experimental, used for engine testing.
How features graduate

A feature gets documented pages when it can be exercised end to end in a released build. Until then it lives here, so the docs never describe behavior you cannot run.

DRAFT
This page is not published. Only visible in development mode.
Routines

Everything callable in ReifyDB is a routine, and there are two kinds. Functions are pure computations used inside expressions; procedures run statements, may write, and are invoked with CALL. Both answer to the same namespace::name addressing. This page is the map; the linked references go deep.

One naming scheme

Every routine has a qualified name of the form namespace::name - math::abs, text::upper, clock::now. Built-ins register under fixed namespaces when the engine boots, and those names are stable: they appear in stored queries, scripts, and client code, so ReifyDB adds new routines rather than renaming existing ones. Your own procedures live in your own namespaces, addressed exactly like your tables.

Variants of a function are separate flat names with underscore suffixes, not deeper namespaces: the saturating sibling of math::add is math::add_saturate, and the none-on-overflow one is math::add_none. The arithmetic policy suffixes (_saturate, _wrap, _zero, _none, _default, _strict) are the largest such family; see Arithmetic Policies.

Functions: values in, values out

Functions are pure. They transform input values into output values and touch nothing else, which is what makes them safe anywhere an expression is expected: in map, filter, extend, aggregate. Nearly 200 built-ins ship in domain modules - math, text, date, datetime, duration, time, blob, json, is, and more - each documented in the function reference.

$ Functions Compute Values in Expressions
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
3
4
MAP {
  distance: math::abs(-42),
  label: text::upper('reify')
}
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run

Most functions are scalar: one output value per row. Some are aggregates - math::sum, math::avg, math::count - and fold a whole group into one value inside aggregate. A few are generators that produce rows instead of transforming them, such as series::generate standing as a FROM source.

Procedures: statements with a name

Procedures are imperative. A procedure body runs statements, can read and write data, and is invoked as a statement of its own with CALL. Define one with CREATE PROCEDURE: it is stored in the catalog next to your data, takes typed parameters, and references them as $param in the body.

$ Procedures Run Statements with CALL
Expand
Copy
Reset
1
2
3
4
5
6
7
8
9
CREATE NAMESPACE rt_ov;
CREATE TABLE rt_ov::audit { note: utf8 };
CREATE PROCEDURE rt_ov::log { note: utf8 } AS {
  INSERT rt_ov::audit [{ note: $note }]
};
CALL rt_ov::log('first entry');
CALL rt_ov::log('second entry');
FROM rt_ov::audit
SORT { note: asc }
$ ctrl+enter to run
Run

Built-in procedures answer to the same naming scheme and call syntax: clock::set and clock::advance steer the engine clock in tests, rql::explain inspects query plans. See Procedures for definitions and Parameters & Control Flow for conditionals and loops inside bodies.

Request-scoped functions

RQL scripts can also define functions on the fly: anonymous closures bound to variables with let, and named functions declared with udf. These are not registered routines - they exist only for the request that defines them. Reach for them to factor a repeated computation inside one script; reach for a procedure when the logic should be stored in the database and callable by name. See Closures & Functions.

Where to go next
Browse the function reference module by module, read Arithmetic Policies before doing math on untrusted numbers, and start Procedures when application logic should live next to the data it touches.
